images.caption: Figure 1:The risk-under-pressure evaluation framework.An attack trial is initialized with a benchmark behavior, attack policyπ\pi, target modelMM, and query budgetλ\lambda. For each prompt, the budgeted iterative refinement loop generates a response, evaluates it with a safety judge, and either terminates on success or refines the prompt for the next step. Trial traces are then re-parameterized by cumulativeFLOPto produce risk-compute curves, from which two scalar summary metrics are derived:𝒞@​τ\mathcal{C}_{@\tau}(compute toτ%\tau\%risk) and average efficiency (ae), the normalized expected risk perFLOP.

Figure 2:Effect of training stage on adversarial robustness (HarmBench).Risk-compute curves forTulu3-8B(Base, SFT, DPO, RLVR) under GCG (left), PAIR(center), and JailBroken (right). Among aligned models, SFT achieves the greatest robustness; further alignment via DPO or RLVR reduces robustness relative to SFT. The ranking across stages is consistent across attack types, though the separating compute cost varies substantially.

Figure 3:Effect of model size on adversarial robustness (HarmBench).Risk-compute curves forQwen 2.5-Instruct(0.5B, 3B, 7B) under GCG
(left) and JailBroken (center), with JailBroken summary metrics (right). Dashed
lines mark𝒞@​0.5\mathcal{C}_{@0.5}(compute to 50% risk); red arrows show the multiplicative increase in compute cost from
0.5B to 7B. Bars report ASR @λ=10\lambda{=}10query budget (solid) and AE
(×10−3\times 10^{-3}risk/tflop, hatched). Larger models demand substantially
more compute to breach (20×20\timesunder GCG,2.8×2.8\timesunder JailBroken), yet
ASR remains similar across scales. Scaling benefits are not uniform across attack
types.

Figure 4:Effect of surrogate-to-target attack transfer on adversarial robustness (HarmBench).Risk-compute curves for the source model (light) and transfer target (dark) under GCG. Each point corresponds to a query budgetλ\lambda; arrows connect matchedλ\lambdavalues across curves, showing that suffixes optimized on the open-weight surrogate (Qwen2.5-0.5B) at a given budget transfer to the closed-weight target (Qwen3-8B) under the same budget. Risk rises quickly before plateauing at the target, suggesting the ceiling is governed by suffix quality and target robustness (not additional compute), a ceiling fixed-budget ASR may miss entirely.

Figure 5:Effect of safety alignment on adversarial robustness (HarmBench).Risk-compute curves forQwen3-4BandQwen3-4B-SafeRLunder GCG (left), PAIR (center), and JailBroken (right). Safety-RL increases vulnerability under white-box attack: the SafeRL model incurs strictly higher risk than the base at every compute level under GCG. Under iterative prompt optimization (PAIR), alignment confers modest but inconsistent protection. Against template-based attack (JailBroken), the two models converge to nearly identical risk, leaving the attack surface largely intact.

Figure 6:Uniformity of adversarial compute cost across harm categories under JailBroken (HarmBench).C@​0.5C_{@0.5}(left) and AE (right) broken down by harm category forQwen3-4BandQwen3-4B-SafeRL. Categories are ordered byQwen3-4B-SafeRL’s performance on each respective metric. Category abbreviations:Harassment: harassment & bullying;Misinformation: misinformation & disinformation;Harmful: general harm;Illegal: illegal activities;Chem. & Bio.: chemical & biological weapons/drugs;Cybercrime: cybercrime & unauthorized intrusion. Safety-RL improves robustness unevenly across harm categories; Safety-RL can also backfire, increasing per-TFLOP exploitability in cybercrime, illegal, and misinformation relative to the base model.

Figure 8:Uniformity of adversarial compute cost across harm categories under JailBroken (JailbreakBench).C@​0.5C_{@0.5}(left) and AE (right) broken down by harm category forQwen3-4BandQwen3-4B-SafeRL.
